diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The migratory pattern (visceral T10 pain localising to somatic RLQ) is characteristic of appendicitis.
Gastroenteritis pain is typically diffuse and crampy, not migratory and localising.
Anorexia is a sensitive (if non-specific) early feature; its absence should make one reconsider appendicitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Nausea and vomiting occurring after the pain onset

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pain-preceding-vomiting favours appendicitis; vomiting-preceding-pain favours gastroenteritis.
The pain-first sequence is atypical for gastroenteritis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain worse with movement, coughing and jolting (peritonism)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Movement/cough-sensitive pain indicates parietal peritoneal irritation — a hallmark of localised peritonitis.
Low-grade fever fits an inflammatory process; high fever/rigors should raise concern for perforation or another source.
A negative test lowers ectopic probability, but a single self-reported test does not fully exclude it — formal beta-hCG is needed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of dysuria, frequency or urgency

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The absence of lower urinary tract symptoms makes UTI an unlikely explanation for the pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Localised rebound tenderness at McBurney's point

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Localised RLQ rebound at McBurney's point is a strong sign of appendiceal peritonitis — sealed clinician finding, not obtainable via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Positive Rovsing's, psoas and obturator signs

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Rovsing's (referred RLQ pain), psoas and obturator signs localise peritoneal irritation to the appendix — sealed clinician manoeuvres.

symptoms.symptom_name: Involuntary guarding and rigidity of the right iliac fossa

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Involuntary guarding indicates established localised peritonitis — cannot be assessed remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Enlarged non-compressible appendix >6 mm with periappendiceal fat stranding on US/CT

investigation_details.expected_result: Non-compressible blind-ending tubular structure >6 mm with periappendiceal inflammatory fat stranding
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Diagnosis unconfirmed; risk of missed or delayed appendicectomy and progression to perforation.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Imaging confirmation of an enlarged non-compressible appendix with fat stranding is diagnostic — hospital investigation, never available via telehealth.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Migration + anorexia + pain-before-vomiting + localised peritonism together give a high pre-test probability of appendicitis and mandate urgent surgical assessment.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Raised WBC and CRP support an inflammatory/infective surgical abdomen and inform surgical decision-making — obtained in the ED, not via telehealth.
Imaging confirms the diagnosis and excludes mimics; US is first-line in a younger female to avoid radiation, with CT if inconclusive.
Formal pregnancy test to exclude ectopic pregnancy in a reproductive-age female before imaging/surgery — a self-reported test is not sufficient.
Helps assess for urinary tract infection and haematuria; mild pyuria can occur in appendicitis from bladder/ureteric irritation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: CT abdomen as a first move in a young female
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Ultrasound is preferred first-line in a younger female to avoid ionising radiation; CT is reserved for inconclusive US — over-ordering CT up front is not the expected first step.
